Effect of a New Inhaled Therapy on Lung Function in Mild Asthma

Author

Natallia Halai

Published

April 28, 2026

Abstract:
A randomized controlled trial evaluates a new inhaled corticosteroid (ICS-X) compared to a standard inhaler in patients with mild persistent asthma. Patients are randomized to receive either ICS-X or standard therapy for 6 weeks. Lung function is assessed at baseline and at follow-up using FEV₁ (forced expiratory volume in 1 second). The primary endpoint is improvement in FEV₁.

1 Hypothesis

  • ICS-X leads to greater improvement in FEV₁

  • Higher baseline FEV₁ is associated with less room for improvement

  • Smokers exhibit a reduced treatment response

  • A negative Age effect on improvement

2 Data Exploration

2.1 Numerically

Code 
cor_baseline<-cor(lung_function$baseline_fev1, lung_function$fev1_improvement)
cor_age<-cor(lung_function$age, lung_function$fev1_improvement)

correlation_table<-data.frame(
  Variable_1=c("Baseline FEV", "Age"),
  Variable_2=c("FEV Improvement", "FEV Improvement"),
  Corr_value=c(cor_baseline, cor_age)
)

knitr::kable(correlation_table)

As the first step in the data exploration correlation values were calculated for the 2 continuous variables Age and Baseline FEV as seen in Table 1 . Here we can partly confirm our hypotheses which state that Age has a negative effect on FEV improvement (corr: -0.2892998 ) as well as high Baseline FEV association with less FEV improvement (corr: -0.5043706).

2.2 Graphically

Code 
ggarrange(
  ggplot(lung_function, aes(x=treatment_group, y=fev1_improvement, color=treatment_group))+
    geom_point()+
    geom_boxplot(outlier.shape = NA)+
    labs(x="Treatment", y="FEV improvement"),
  
  ggplot(lung_function, aes(x=baseline_fev1,y=fev1_improvement, color=treatment_group))+
    geom_point()+
    geom_smooth(method="lm")+
    labs(x="Baseline FEV", y="FEV improvement"),

  ggplot(lung_function, aes(x=smoking_status, y=fev1_improvement))+
    geom_point()+
    geom_boxplot(outlier.shape=NA)+
    labs(x="Smoking Status", y="FEV improvement"),

  ggplot(lung_function, aes(x=age, y=fev1_improvement, color=treatment_group))+
    geom_point()+
    geom_smooth(method="lm")+
    labs(x="Age", y="FEV improvement"),

  common.legend = TRUE, labels=c("a)", "b)", "c)", "d)")
)

In Figure 1 we can see different predictor variables plotted against FEV improvement response variable.

3 T-test for Treatment Effects

Code 
(t_values<-t.test(fev1_improvement~treatment_group, lung_function))

The two sample t-test shows that there seems to be a statistically significant difference between the two treatment groups. (p =0.0080771, 95% CI = (-0.1582247, -0.0239975) )

4 Model Fitting

4.1 Create Candidate Models

We will be choosing the model.04 which has all the predictor variables included as it explains the most variance in the data.

4.2 Check chosen model diagnostics

Code 
check_model(model.04)

In Figure 2 we can see that there is no high collinearity between the variables, the homoscedasticity assumption is also met as well as the Normality (normal residuals distribution) and Linearity(trends in the residuals). Also we can detect no outliers/influencial points in the Cook’s plot.

4.3 Interpretting the chosen model

5 Conclusion

A Linear Model (R2= 0.4697638, \(F-test_{( 5 , 174 )}\)= 32.717, p= < 0.001; Table 3) performed significantly better than a null model, compromising an intercept only.

We generated compelling support for the following Hypothesis:

  1. ICS-X leads to greater improvement in FEV₁

    • A two sided t-test confirmed that there is indeed a difference in effect between the two treatment groups. (p =0.0080771, 95% CI = (-0.1582247, -0.0239975) )
    • And in the Table 3 we can see that the ICS-X treatment had a significant positive effect on the post-treatment FEV. (B1: 0.063, p = 0.016)

  2. Higher baseline FEV₁ is associated with less room for improvement

    • As seen in the Figure 3 and also in the Table 3 Baseline FEV seems to be negatively correlated with the FEV improvement. This effects is also statistically significant. (B1: -0.182, p < 0.001)

  3. Smokers exhibit a reduced treatment response

    • In our exploration Figure 1 we could already see that this hypothesis must already be rejected and after completing a full analysis we can conclude that smokers profit from the treatment more than non-smokers and former smokers as shown by their positive slope in Table 3 while keeping other variables constant.

  4. A negative Age effect on improvement

    • Age as shown in Table 3 and in previous figures (Figure 3, Figure 1) does have a negative effect on FEV improvement which is also shown to be statistically significant. (B1: -0.009, p < 0.001)

Accounting all of these points we have enough evidence to accept all our prior hypotheses excluding the 3rd one as we have seen that smokers have a higher FEV improvement which can be explained with the same reasoning as we did with Baseline FEV, meaning that non-smokers have already a higher Baseline thus the treatment effect is only small compared to smokers.
